title: Legal & Ethical — Torts, Ethics & Patient Rights (Set 2)
subtitle: Assault/negligence, ethical principles, boundaries, advance directives, abuse reporting & errors
scenario: Legal concepts (torts), ethical principles, professional boundaries, advance directives, mandated reporting, and safe documentation for the psychiatric technician.

steps.q: Threatening a client by saying 'Take this medication or I'll hold you down,' which makes the client fear imminent harmful contact, is an example of:

opts.text: Assault
opts.rat: Assault is an act that creates a reasonable fear of imminent harmful or offensive contact; actual contact is not required (that would be battery).

opts.text: Negligence
opts.rat: Negligence is failing to meet the standard of care, not threatening contact.

opts.text: Defamation
opts.rat: Defamation involves false statements harming reputation.

opts.text: Beneficence
opts.rat: Beneficence is an ethical principle of doing good, not a threat.

steps.q: Failing to raise the side rails per the care plan, resulting in a client's fall, is an example of:

opts.text: Assault
opts.rat: Assault involves threatened contact, not a failure to act.

opts.text: Negligence
opts.rat: Negligence is failing to act as a reasonably prudent person would, causing harm to the client.

opts.text: Battery
opts.rat: Battery is unconsented contact, not an omission of care.

opts.text: Slander
opts.rat: Slander is spoken defamation, unrelated to a fall.

steps.q: Malpractice is BEST described as:

opts.text: Any honest mistake with no consequences
opts.rat: Malpractice specifically involves professional negligence causing harm.

opts.text: A threat of contact
opts.rat: That describes assault, not malpractice.

opts.text: Professional negligence by a licensed provider that harms a client
opts.rat: Malpractice is negligence committed by a professional acting in their licensed role, resulting in client harm.

opts.text: A type of advance directive
opts.rat: This is unrelated to malpractice.

steps.q: The ethical principle of AUTONOMY means the technician should:

opts.text: Always decide what is best for the client without their input
opts.rat: Deciding for the client overrides autonomy.

opts.text: Tell the client only what staff want them to hear
opts.rat: Withholding information undermines autonomous choice.

opts.text: Ignore the client's preferences
opts.rat: Ignoring preferences violates autonomy.

opts.text: Respect the client's right to make their own decisions
opts.rat: Autonomy is respecting the client's right to self-determination and to make their own informed choices.

steps.q: The ethical principle of NONMALEFICENCE requires the technician to:

opts.text: Do no harm to the client
opts.rat: Nonmaleficence is the duty to avoid causing harm to the client.

opts.text: Always tell the truth
opts.rat: That describes veracity, not nonmaleficence.

opts.text: Distribute resources fairly
opts.rat: That describes justice.

opts.text: Keep all promises
opts.rat: That describes fidelity.

steps.q: The ethical principle of VERACITY is demonstrated when the technician:

opts.text: Avoids harm
opts.rat: That is nonmaleficence.

opts.text: Is truthful and honest with the client
opts.rat: Veracity is the duty to tell the truth and communicate honestly with clients.

opts.text: Does good for the client
opts.rat: That is beneficence.

opts.text: Treats everyone equally
opts.rat: That is justice.

steps.q: Maintaining professional boundaries means the technician should:

opts.text: Accept money and gifts from clients
opts.rat: Accepting gifts/money from clients crosses professional boundaries.

opts.text: Share personal problems with clients for support
opts.rat: Using clients for personal support is a boundary violation.

opts.text: Keep the relationship therapeutic and avoid personal/dual relationships and gifts
opts.rat: Professional boundaries keep the relationship therapeutic; accepting gifts or forming personal/dual relationships blurs boundaries and is inappropriate.

opts.text: Socialize with clients outside the facility
opts.rat: Personal relationships outside the therapeutic role violate boundaries.

steps.q: A durable power of attorney for health care is a type of advance directive that:

opts.text: Gives a stranger control of the client's bank account
opts.rat: This describes financial control, not a health-care advance directive.

opts.text: Cancels all of the client's rights
opts.rat: It does not remove the client's rights; it supports their wishes.

opts.text: Is only about funeral planning
opts.rat: It concerns health-care decision-making, not funerals.

opts.text: Names a person to make health-care decisions if the client cannot
opts.rat: A durable power of attorney for health care designates a surrogate decision-maker to act on the client's behalf if they lose capacity.

steps.q: The difference between capacity and competency is that competency is:

opts.text: A legal determination made by a court
opts.rat: Competency is a legal status decided by a court, whereas capacity is a clinical judgment of the client's ability to understand and make a specific decision.

opts.text: The same thing as capacity
opts.rat: They are related but distinct concepts.

opts.text: Decided by the technician alone
opts.rat: Competency is a court determination, not made by direct-care staff.

opts.text: Based only on the client's age
opts.rat: Competency is not determined by age alone.

steps.q: Under California patient-rights law, clients on a psychiatric unit generally have the right to:

opts.text: Never contact anyone outside the facility
opts.rat: Reasonable access to phones and visitors is a protected right.

opts.text: Wear their own clothing, keep personal possessions, and have reasonable access to phones, visitors, and mail
opts.rat: Enumerated patient rights include wearing one's own clothing, keeping personal possessions, and reasonable access to phones, visitors, and mail—deniable only for good cause, documented.

opts.text: Have all belongings permanently confiscated
opts.rat: Confiscating belongings without good cause violates patient rights.

opts.text: No rights while hospitalized
opts.rat: Clients retain enumerated rights during hospitalization.

steps.q: A client with capacity refuses electroconvulsive therapy (ECT). The technician understands the client:

opts.text: Must receive ECT because they are hospitalized
opts.rat: Hospitalization does not remove the right to refuse ECT.

opts.text: Can be given ECT without any consent
opts.rat: ECT requires consent or legal authorization.

opts.text: Has the right to refuse ECT; involuntary ECT requires a legal process
opts.rat: Clients have the right to refuse ECT; administering it involuntarily requires specific legal authorization, not staff decision.

opts.text: Loses all rights by refusing
opts.rat: Refusing treatment does not strip a client of rights.

steps.q: After a client falls, the technician completes an incident (unusual occurrence) report. This report is:

opts.text: A place to assign blame to a coworker
opts.rat: Incident reports are not for blaming individuals.

opts.text: Optional and rarely completed
opts.rat: Reporting unusual occurrences is required, not optional.

opts.text: Filed only if the family complains
opts.rat: It is completed based on the event, not only on complaints.

opts.text: A factual record of the event used for quality/risk review, kept separate from the chart
opts.rat: An incident report factually documents an unusual occurrence for risk management and quality improvement and is kept separate from the medical record.

steps.q: When the technician makes a documentation error in the paper chart, the correct action is to:

opts.text: Draw a single line through it, write 'error,' and initial and date it
opts.rat: The proper legal correction is a single line through the error with 'error,' initials, and date—never erasing, obliterating, or using correction fluid.

opts.text: Use correction fluid to cover it completely
opts.rat: Obscuring an entry looks like tampering and is not allowed.

opts.text: Erase the entry entirely
opts.rat: Erasing destroys the legal record and is prohibited.

opts.text: Scribble it out until unreadable
opts.rat: Making an entry unreadable is improper and raises tampering concerns.

steps.q: A technician notices a client's relative is taking the client's Social Security money without permission. This is a form of abuse known as:

opts.text: A normal family arrangement
opts.rat: Taking a client's money without permission is exploitation, not a normal arrangement.

opts.text: Financial exploitation
opts.rat: Financial (material) exploitation is misuse of a dependent adult's funds or property and is a reportable form of abuse.

opts.text: Beneficence
opts.rat: This is harmful exploitation, not an ethical good.

opts.text: Confidentiality
opts.rat: This is unrelated to confidentiality.

steps.q: As a mandated reporter, if a technician has a reasonable suspicion of child abuse, they must:

opts.text: Wait until they have absolute proof
opts.rat: Reporting is based on reasonable suspicion, not proof.

opts.text: Only report if the parent admits it
opts.rat: A confession is not required to report.

opts.text: Report it promptly to the proper authorities per law
opts.rat: Mandated reporters must report reasonable suspicion of child abuse promptly to the appropriate authorities; proof is not required.

opts.text: Keep it confidential to protect the family
opts.rat: The legal duty to report overrides confidentiality here.

steps.q: A technician observes a coworker appearing intoxicated while caring for clients. The MOST appropriate action is to:

opts.text: Say nothing to avoid conflict
opts.rat: Ignoring impairment endangers clients.

opts.text: Cover the coworker's mistakes
opts.rat: Concealing impairment increases risk to clients.

opts.text: Confront the coworker's family
opts.rat: This does not address client safety through proper channels.

opts.text: Report the concern to the supervisor to protect client safety
opts.rat: An impaired coworker endangers clients; the technician has a duty to report the concern through proper channels to protect client safety.

steps.q: Client confidentiality may be legally broken in which situation?

opts.text: A serious threat to an identifiable person, suspected abuse, or a court order
opts.rat: Confidentiality yields to a duty to protect (e.g., Tarasoff), mandated abuse reporting, and lawful court orders.

opts.text: Because a coworker is simply curious
opts.rat: Curiosity is not a lawful exception to confidentiality.

opts.text: To share gossip with friends
opts.rat: Sharing for gossip is a serious violation.

opts.text: To post about the client on social media
opts.rat: Posting client information is never permitted.

steps.q: Consent to treat a minor child is generally provided by:

opts.text: Any adult on the unit
opts.rat: Only a parent/legal guardian (or legally authorized person) may consent.

opts.text: The parent or legal guardian, except in defined emergency or special situations
opts.rat: A parent or legal guardian typically consents for a minor, except in emergencies or specific legally defined circumstances.

opts.text: The technician
opts.rat: Direct-care staff do not provide consent for a minor.

opts.text: The minor's friends
opts.rat: Friends have no legal authority to consent.

steps.q: In California, before an involuntarily held client can be given antipsychotic medication over their objection (non-emergency), there must be:

opts.text: Only the technician's decision
opts.rat: Direct-care staff cannot authorize forced medication.

opts.text: A family member's verbal okay
opts.rat: A relative's informal approval does not authorize forced non-emergency medication.

opts.text: A capacity determination (Riese hearing) finding the client unable to consent
opts.rat: Outside an emergency, forced antipsychotic medication of a held client requires a capacity determination (Riese hearing); the hold alone is not enough.

opts.text: Nothing; the hold allows any medication by force
opts.rat: A hold does not by itself authorize forced non-emergency antipsychotics.

steps.q: If a technician realizes they gave a client the wrong medication, the FIRST priority is to:

opts.text: Hide the error to avoid getting in trouble
opts.rat: Concealing a medication error endangers the client and is unethical and illegal.

opts.text: Chart that the correct medication was given
opts.rat: Falsifying the record is illegal and dangerous.

opts.text: Wait until the end of the shift to mention it
opts.rat: Delaying reporting risks client harm; errors are reported immediately.

opts.text: Check on the client's safety and report the error to the nurse immediately
opts.rat: Client safety comes first: assess the client, notify the nurse/prescriber at once, then document factually and complete an incident report. Errors must be reported honestly, never hidden.
